openapi: 3.1.0
info:
  title: Agentic Control Plane API
  version: 0.2.0
  summary: Policy, approval, execution, and audit APIs for governed agent actions
  description: |
    v0.2 extends the base control-plane lifecycle with richer, domain-specific schemas for:
      - Git and pull request actions
      - Deploy and cloud actions
      - Email and calendar actions
      - Payment and procurement actions

    Core lifecycle:
      intent -> policy decision -> approval (optional) -> execution grant -> execution -> trace

servers:
  - url: https://api.agentic-control-plane.example.com
    description: Production
  - url: https://sandbox.agentic-control-plane.example.com
    description: Sandbox

security:
  - bearerAuth: []

tags:
  - name: Intents
  - name: Approvals
  - name: Policies
  - name: Connectors
  - name: Executions
  - name: Funding Sources
  - name: Credentials
  - name: Traces
  - name: Analytics
  - name: Health

paths:
  /v1/health:
    get:
      tags: [Health]
      summary: Get service health
      operationId: getHealth
      security: []
      responses:
        "200":
          description: Service health
          content:
            application/json:
              schema:
                $ref: "#/components/schemas/HealthResponse"

  /v1/intents:
    post:
      tags: [Intents]
      summary: Submit a new action intent
      operationId: createIntent
      requestBody:
        required: true
        content:
          application/json:
            schema:
              $ref: "#/components/schemas/CreateIntentRequest"
            examples:
              gitPullRequest:
                summary: Create a pull request
                value:
                  idempotency_key: "REDACTED"
                  agent_id: "agt_codex_repo_alpha"
                  workspace_id: "ws_engineering"
                  action:
                    type: "git.pull_request.create"
                    summary: "Open PR from feature branch to main"
                    justification: "Ship checkout bug fix"
                    target:
                      kind: "repository"
                      id: "repo_checkout"
                      display_name: "github.com/acme/checkout"
                    domain:
                      git:
                        provider: "github"
                        repository: "acme/checkout"
                        branch: "fix/checkout-tax-rounding"
                        base_branch: "main"
                        commit_shas:
                          - "abc1234"
                        files_changed:
                          - "src/tax.ts"
                          - "tests/tax.test.ts"
                        pr_title: "Fix tax rounding on checkout"
                        pr_body: "This PR fixes checkout tax rounding and adds regression tests."
                        labels: ["bugfix", "checkout"]
                  context:
                    environment: "production"
                    source_platform: "codex"
              paymentPurchase:
                summary: Purchase SaaS subscription
                value:
                  idempotency_key: "REDACTED"
                  agent_id: "agt_procurement_ops"
                  workspace_id: "ws_finops"
                  action:
                    type: "payment.charge"
                    summary: "Purchase annual Figma license"
                    justification: "Renew expiring seat for jane@company.com"
                    target:
                      kind: "vendor"
                      id: "vendor_figma"
                      display_name: "Figma"
                    domain:
                      payment:
                        vendor_name: "Figma"
                        amount:
                          amount: 18000
                          currency: "USD"
                        merchant_category: "software"
                        budget_code: "design-software"
                        funding_source_id: "fs_team_software"
                        payment_instrument_preference: "virtual_card"
                        line_items:
                          - description: "Figma Pro Annual"
                            quantity: 1
                            unit_amount:
                              amount: 18000
                              currency: "USD"
                        beneficiary:
                          email: "jane@company.com"
                  context:
                    environment: "production"
                    source_platform: "openclaw"
      responses:
        "201":
          description: Intent created
          content:
            application/json:
              schema:
                $ref: "#/components/schemas/IntentEnvelope"

    get:
      tags: [Intents]
      summary: List intents
      operationId: listIntents
      parameters:
        - $ref: "#/components/parameters/PageSize"
        - $ref: "#/components/parameters/PageToken"
        - name: workspace_id
          in: query
          schema:
            type: string
        - name: status
          in: query
          schema:
            $ref: "#/components/schemas/IntentStatus"
        - name: action_type
          in: query
          schema:
            type: string
        - name: agent_id
          in: query
          schema:
            type: string
      responses:
        "200":
          description: Paginated intents
          content:
            application/json:
              schema:
                $ref: "#/components/schemas/IntentListResponse"

  /v1/intents/{intent_id}:
    get:
      tags: [Intents]
      summary: Get an intent
      operationId: getIntent
      parameters:
        - $ref: "#/components/parameters/IntentId"
      responses:
        "200":
          description: Intent details
          content:
            application/json:
              schema:
                $ref: "#/components/schemas/IntentEnvelope"

  /v1/intents/{intent_id}/evidence:
    post:
      tags: [Intents]
      summary: Attach evidence to an intent
      operationId: appendIntentEvidence
      parameters:
        - $ref: "#/components/parameters/IntentId"
      requestBody:
        required: true
        content:
          application/json:
            schema:
              $ref: "#/components/schemas/AppendEvidenceRequest"
      responses:
        "200":
          description: Evidence appended
          content:
            application/json:
              schema:
                $ref: "#/components/schemas/IntentEnvelope"

  /v1/approvals:
    get:
      tags: [Approvals]
      summary: List approval requests
      operationId: listApprovals
      parameters:
        - $ref: "#/components/parameters/PageSize"
        - $ref: "#/components/parameters/PageToken"
        - name: assignee
          in: query
          schema:
            type: string
      responses:
        "200":
          description: Approval queue
          content:
            application/json:
              schema:
                $ref: "#/components/schemas/ApprovalListResponse"

  /v1/approvals/{approval_id}:
    get:
      tags: [Approvals]
      summary: Get an approval request
      operationId: getApproval
      parameters:
        - $ref: "#/components/parameters/ApprovalId"
      responses:
        "200":
          description: Approval details
          content:
            application/json:
              schema:
                $ref: "#/components/schemas/Approval"

  /v1/approvals/{approval_id}/approve:
    post:
      tags: [Approvals]
      summary: Approve an action
      operationId: approveRequest
      parameters:
        - $ref: "#/components/parameters/ApprovalId"
      requestBody:
        required: false
        content:
          application/json:
            schema:
              $ref: "#/components/schemas/ApproveRequest"
      responses:
        "200":
          description: Approval recorded
          content:
            application/json:
              schema:
                $ref: "#/components/schemas/ApprovalActionResponse"

  /v1/approvals/{approval_id}/deny:
    post:
      tags: [Approvals]
      summary: Deny an action
      operationId: denyRequest
      parameters:
        - $ref: "#/components/parameters/ApprovalId"
      requestBody:
        required: true
        content:
          application/json:
            schema:
              $ref: "#/components/schemas/DenyRequest"
      responses:
        "200":
          description: Denial recorded
          content:
            application/json:
              schema:
                $ref: "#/components/schemas/ApprovalActionResponse"

  /v1/approvals/{approval_id}/modify:
    post:
      tags: [Approvals]
      summary: Approve with modifications
      operationId: modifyRequest
      parameters:
        - $ref: "#/components/parameters/ApprovalId"
      requestBody:
        required: true
        content:
          application/json:
            schema:
              $ref: "#/components/schemas/ModifyApprovalRequest"
      responses:
        "200":
          description: Modification recorded
          content:
            application/json:

content.content:
1→// Package main is the entry point for the Aileron control plane server.
     2→//
     3→// The server wires together the core components — policy engine, approval
     4→// orchestrator, connector registry, vault, notifiers, and audit store —
     5→// and exposes the control plane API over HTTP.
     6→package main
     7→
     8→import (
     9→	"context"
    10→	"fmt"
    11→	"log/slog"
    12→	"net/http"
    13→	"os"
    14→	"os/signal"
    15→	"syscall"
    16→	"time"
    17→
    18→	"github.com/ALRubinger/aileron/core/connector"
    19→	googlecalendar "github.com/ALRubinger/aileron/core/connector/calendar/google"
    20→	"github.com/ALRubinger/aileron/core/connector/git/github"
    21→	"github.com/ALRubinger/aileron/core/connector/payments/stripe"
    22→)
    23→
    24→func main() {
    25→	log := slog.New(slog.NewJSONHandler(os.Stdout, nil))
    26→
    27→	if err := run(log); err != nil {
    28→		log.Error("server exited with error", "error", err)
    29→		os.Exit(1)
    30→	}
    31→}
    32→
    33→func run(log *slog.Logger) error {
    34→	cfg := configFromEnv()
    35→
    36→	// --- Connector registry ---
    37→	registry := connector.NewRegistry()
    38→	registry.Register(stripe.New())
    39→	registry.Register(googlecalendar.New())
    40→	registry.Register(github.New())
    41→
    42→	// --- HTTP server ---
    43→	mux := http.NewServeMux()
    44→	registerRoutes(mux, log, registry)
    45→
    46→	srv := &http.Server{
    47→		Addr:         cfg.addr,
    48→		Handler:      mux,
    49→		ReadTimeout:  10 * time.Second,
    50→		WriteTimeout: 30 * time.Second,
    51→		IdleTimeout:  120 * time.Second,
    52→	}
    53→
    54→	// Graceful shutdown on SIGINT / SIGTERM.
    55→	ctx, stop := signal.NotifyContext(context.Background(), syscall.SIGINT, syscall.SIGTERM)
    56→	defer stop()
    57→
    58→	go func() {
    59→		log.Info("server listening", "addr", cfg.addr)
    60→		if err := srv.ListenAndServe(); err != nil && err != http.ErrServerClosed {
    61→			log.Error("listen error", "error", err)
    62→		}
    63→	}()
    64→
    65→	<-ctx.Done()
    66→	log.Info("shutting down")
    67→
    68→	shutdownCtx, cancel := context.WithTimeout(context.Background(), 30*time.Second)
    69→	defer cancel()
    70→
    71→	return srv.Shutdown(shutdownCtx)
    72→}
    73→
    74→func registerRoutes(mux *http.ServeMux, log *slog.Logger, _ *connector.Registry) {
    75→	// Health check — no auth required.
    76→	mux.HandleFunc("GET /v1/health", handleHealth(log))
    77→
    78→	// TODO: register intent, approval, policy, connector, execution,
    79→	// funding source, credential, trace, and analytics handlers.
    80→}
    81→
    82→func handleHealth(log *slog.Logger) http.HandlerFunc {
    83→	return func(w http.ResponseWriter, r *http.Request) {
    84→		log.InfoContext(r.Context(), "health check")
    85→		w.Header().Set("Content-Type", "application/json")
    86→		fmt.Fprintf(w, `{"status":"ok","service":"aileron","version":"0.1.0"}`)
    87→	}
    88→}
    89→
    90→// config holds runtime configuration sourced from environment variables.
    91→type config struct {
    92→	addr        string
    93→	databaseURL string
    94→}
    95→
    96→func configFromEnv() config {
    97→	addr := os.Getenv("AILERON_ADDR")
    98→	if addr == "" {
    99→		addr = ":8080"
   100→	}
   101→	return config{
   102→		addr:        addr,
   103→		databaseURL: os.Getenv("DATABASE_URL"),
   104→	}
   105→}
   106→

1→// Package connector defines the SPI for execution connectors.
     2→//
     3→// A connector bridges the control plane to an external system — a payment
     4→// processor, a git provider, a calendar service, etc. When the policy engine
     5→// allows an action (or a human approves it), the control plane selects the
     6→// appropriate connector and calls Execute.
     7→//
     8→// Each connector implementation is responsible for one external system.
     9→// The SPI is intentionally narrow: connectors receive a bounded execution
    10→// request and return a result. Credential injection and parameter bounding
    11→// happen in the control plane before the connector is called.
    12→package connector
    13→
    14→import "context"
    15→
    16→// Connector executes an approved action against an external system.
    17→type Connector interface {
    18→	// Type returns the connector type identifier (e.g. "payments", "git",
    19→	// "calendar"). Used by the registry to route execution requests.
    20→	Type() string
    21→
    22→	// Provider returns the specific provider name (e.g. "stripe", "github",
    23→	// "google_calendar"). Multiple connectors may share a Type.
    24→	Provider() string
    25→
    26→	// Execute runs the action described by the request.
    27→	Execute(ctx context.Context, req ExecutionRequest) (ExecutionResult, error)
    28→}
    29→
    30→// ExecutionRequest is passed to a connector when executing an approved action.
    31→type ExecutionRequest struct {
    32→	GrantID     string
    33→	IntentID    string
    34→	ConnectorID string
    35→	// ActionType is the dot-namespaced action, e.g. "payment.charge".
    36→	ActionType string
    37→	// Parameters are the bounded, approved parameters for execution.
    38→	// These are constrained by the execution grant and may differ from
    39→	// the original intent if a human modified them during approval.
    40→	Parameters map[string]any
    41→	// Credential is the injected secret for this connector, resolved from
    42→	// the vault. Connectors must not store or log this value.
    43→	Credential *InjectedCredential
    44→}
    45→
    46→// InjectedCredential carries a resolved secret for use during execution.
    47→// Connectors receive this transiently and must not persist it.
    48→type InjectedCredential struct {
    49→	Type  string
    50→	Value []byte
    51→}
    52→
    53→// ExecutionResult is returned by a connector after execution.
    54→type ExecutionResult struct {
    55→	Status     ExecutionStatus
    56→	Output     map[string]any
    57→	ReceiptRef string
    58→	Error      string
    59→}
    60→
    61→// ExecutionStatus is the outcome of a connector execution.
    62→type ExecutionStatus string
    63→
    64→const (
    65→	ExecutionStatusSucceeded ExecutionStatus = "succeeded"
    66→	ExecutionStatusFailed    ExecutionStatus = "failed"
    67→	ExecutionStatusCancelled ExecutionStatus = "cancelled"
    68→)
    69→
    70→// Registry holds the registered connectors and routes execution requests.
    71→type Registry struct {
    72→	connectors map[string]Connector
    73→}
    74→
    75→// NewRegistry returns an empty connector registry.
    76→func NewRegistry() *Registry {
    77→	return &Registry{connectors: make(map[string]Connector)}
    78→}
    79→
    80→// Register adds a connector to the registry.
    81→// The key is "<type>/<provider>", e.g. "payments/stripe".
    82→func (r *Registry) Register(c Connector) {
    83→	key := c.Type() + "/" + c.Provider()
    84→	r.connectors[key] = c
    85→}
    86→
    87→// Get returns the connector for the given type and provider.
    88→func (r *Registry) Get(connectorType, provider string) (Connector, bool) {
    89→	c, ok := r.connectors[connectorType+"/"+provider]
    90→	return c, ok
    91→}
    92→

   771→

   775→      properties:
   776→        kind:

   778→          enum: [vendor, repository, environment, api, secret, calendar, email_recipient, ticket, cloud_resource, custom]
   779→        id: { type: string }
   780→        display_name: { type: string }

   785→      properties:

      tags: [Funding Sources]
      summary: Create a funding source
      operationId: createFundingSource
      requestBody:
        required: true
        content:
          application/json:
            schema:
              $ref: "#/components/schemas/CreateFundingSourceRequest"
      responses:
        "201":
          description: Funding source created
          content:
            application/json:
              schema:
                $ref: "#/components/schemas/FundingSource"

  /v1/credentials:
    get:
      tags: [Credentials]
      summary: List credential references
      operationId: listCredentials
      parameters:
        - $ref: "#/components/parameters/PageSize"
        - $ref: "#/components/parameters/PageToken"
      responses:
        "200":
          description: Credentials
          content:
            application/json:
              schema:
                $ref: "#/components/schemas/CredentialListResponse"

    post:
      tags: [Credentials]
      summary: Register a credential reference
      operationId: createCredential
      requestBody:
        required: true
        content:
          application/json:
            schema:
              $ref: "#/components/schemas/CreateCredentialRequest"
      responses:
        "201":
          description: Credential created
          content:
            application/json:
              schema:
                $ref: "#/components/schemas/CredentialReference"

  /v1/traces:
    get:
      tags: [Traces]
      summary: List traces
      operationId: listTraces
      parameters:
        - $ref: "#/components/parameters/PageSize"
        - $ref: "#/components/parameters/PageToken"
      responses:
        "200":
          description: Traces
          content:
            application/json:
              schema:
                $ref: "#/components/schemas/TraceListResponse"

  /v1/analytics/summary:
    get:
      tags: [Analytics]
      summary: Get analytics summary
      operationId: getAnalyticsSummary
      parameters:
        - name: workspace_id
          in: query
          required: true
          schema:
            type: string
        - name: from
          in: query
          required: true
          schema:
            type: string
            format: date-time
        - name: to
          in: query
          required: true
          schema:
            type: string
            format: date-time
      responses:
        "200":
          description: Analytics summary
          content:
            application/json:
              schema:
                $ref: "#/components/schemas/AnalyticsSummary"

components:
  securitySchemes:
    bearerAuth:
      type: http
      scheme: bearer
      bearerFormat: JWT

  parameters:
    IntentId:
      name: intent_id
      in: path
      required: true
      schema: { type: string }
    ApprovalId:
      name: approval_id
      in: path
      required: true
      schema: { type: string }
    PolicyId:
      name: policy_id
      in: path
      required: true
      schema: { type: string }
    ConnectorId:
      name: connector_id
      in: path
      required: true
      schema: { type: string }
    GrantId:
      name: grant_id
      in: path
      required: true
      schema: { type: string }
    ExecutionId:
      name: execution_id
      in: path
      required: true
      schema: { type: string }
    PageSize:
      name: page_size
      in: query
      schema:
        type: integer
        minimum: 1
        maximum: 200
        default: 50
    PageToken:
      name: page_token
      in: query
      schema:
        type: string

  schemas:
    HealthResponse:
      type: object
      required: [status, service, version, timestamp]
      properties:
        status:
          type: string
          enum: [ok, degraded, down]
        service:
          type: string
        version:
          type: string
        timestamp:
          type: string
          format: date-time

    Error:
      type: object
      required: [error]
      properties:
        error:
          type: object
          required: [code, message]
          properties:
            code: { type: string }
            message: { type: string }
            details:
              type: array
              items:
                type: object
                additionalProperties: true
            request_id: { type: string }

    Pagination:
      type: object
      properties:
        next_page_token:
          type: string
          nullable: true

    IntentStatus:
      type: string
      enum: [pending_policy, pending_approval, approved, denied, executing, succeeded, failed, cancelled, expired]

    ApprovalStatus:
      type: string
      enum: [pending, approved, denied, modified, delegated, expired, cancelled]

    PolicyStatus:
      type: string
      enum: [draft, active, archived]

    ConnectorType:
      type: string
      enum: [payments, source_control, cloud, deployment, email, calendar, ticketing, crm, erp, secret_manager, internal_api, custom]

    RiskLevel:
      type: string
      enum: [low, medium, high, critical]

    DecisionDisposition:
      type: string
      enum: [allow, allow_modified, require_approval, deny, require_more_evidence]

    ActorRef:
      type: object
      required: [id, type]
      properties:
        id: { type: string }
        type:
          type: string
          enum: [agent, human, service, connector_runtime]
        display_name: { type: string }

    ActionTarget:
      type: object
      required: [kind]
      properties:
        kind:
          type: string
          enum: [vendor, repository, environment, api, secret, calendar, email_recipient, ticket, cloud_resource, custom]
        id: { type: string }
        display_name: { type: string }

    Money:
      type: object
      required: [amount, currency]
      properties:
        amount:
          type: integer
          description: Amount in minor units
          minimum: 0
        currency:
          type: string
          minLength: 3
          maxLength: 3

    Recipient:
      type: object
      required: [email]
      properties:
        name: { type: string }

        tags:
          type: object
          additionalProperties:
            type: string

    EmailAction:
      type: object
      properties:
        from:
          $ref: "#/components/schemas/Recipient"
        to:
          type: array
          items:
            $ref: "#/components/schemas/Recipient"
        cc:
          type: array
          items:
            $ref: "#/components/schemas/Recipient"
        bcc:
          type: array
          items:
            $ref: "#/components/schemas/Recipient"
        subject:
          type: string
        body_text:
          type: string
        body_html:
          type: string
        attachments:
          type: array
          items:
            $ref: "#/components/schemas/AttachmentRef"
        thread_ref:
          type: string
        send_mode:
          type: string
          enum: [send_now, draft_only]

    CalendarAction:
      type: object
      properties:
        provider:
          type: string
          enum: [google_calendar, outlook, custom]
        title:
          type: string
        description:
          type: string
        attendees:
          type: array
          items:
            $ref: "#/components/schemas/CalendarAttendee"
        start_time:
          type: string
          format: date-time
        end_time:
          type: string
          format: date-time
        timezone:
          type: string
        location:
          type: string
        conference_type:
          type: string
          enum: [none, google_meet, zoom, teams, custom]
        calendar_id:
          type: string
        visibility:
          type: string
          enum: [default, public, private]

    PaymentBeneficiary:
      type: object
      properties:
        name: { type: string }
        email:
          type: string
          format: email
        department: { type: string }

    PaymentAction:
      type: object
      properties:
        vendor_name:
          type: string
        vendor_id:
          type: string
        amount:
          $ref: "#/components/schemas/Money"
        merchant_category:
          type: string
        budget_code:
          type: string
        funding_source_id:
          type: string
        payment_instrument_preference:
          type: string
          enum: [virtual_card, ach, wallet, network_proxy, unspecified]
        beneficiary:
          $ref: "#/components/schemas/PaymentBeneficiary"
        line_items:
          type: array
          items:
            $ref: "#/components/schemas/LineItem"
        renewal:
          type: boolean
        contract_term:
          type: string
        recurring_interval:
          type: string
          enum: [none, monthly, quarterly, annual]
        merchant_reference:
          type: string

    ProcurementAction:
      type: object
      properties:
        request_type:
          type: string
          enum: [software, travel, equipment, contractor, services, other]
        vendor_name:
          type: string
        amount_estimate:
          $ref: "#/components/schemas/Money"
        justification:
          type: string
        requestor:
          type: string
        cost_center:
          type: string
        legal_review_required:
          type: boolean
        security_review_required:
          type: boolean
        line_items:
          type: array
          items:
            $ref: "#/components/schemas/LineItem"

    DomainAction:
      type: object
      properties:
        git:
          $ref: "#/components/schemas/GitAction"
        deploy:
          $ref: "#/components/schemas/DeployAction"
        cloud:
          $ref: "#/components/schemas/CloudAction"
        email:
          $ref: "#/components/schemas/EmailAction"
        calendar:
          $ref: "#/components/schemas/CalendarAction"
        payment:
          $ref: "#/components/schemas/PaymentAction"
        procurement:
          $ref: "#/components/schemas/ProcurementAction"

    ActionIntent:
      type: object
      required: [type, summary]
      properties:
        type:
          type: string
          examples:
            - git.pull_request.create
            - deploy.release
            - cloud.resource.mutate
            - email.send
            - calendar.event.create
            - payment.charge
            - procurement.request.submit
        summary:
          type: string
        justification:
          type: string
        target:
          $ref: "#/components/schemas/ActionTarget"
        domain:
          $ref: "#/components/schemas/DomainAction"
        metadata:
          type: object
          additionalProperties: true

    IntentContext:
      type: object
      properties:
        environment:
          type: string
          examples: [development, staging, production]
        source_platform:
          type: string
          examples: [openclaw, claude_code, codex, openhands, goose, cline, continue, langgraph, custom]
        source_session_id:
          type: string
        source_trace_id:
          type: string
        ip_address:
          type: string
        risk_hints:
          type: array
          items:
            type: string
        user_present:
          type: boolean
        temporary_grant_id:
          type: string

    CreateIntentRequest:
      type: object
      required: [idempotency_key, agent_id, workspace_id, action]
      properties:
        idempotency_key:
          type: string
        agent_id:
          type: string
        workspace_id:
          type: string
        action:
          $ref: "#/components/schemas/ActionIntent"
        context:
          $ref: "#/components/schemas/IntentContext"

    EvidenceItem:
      type: object
      required: [type, value]
      properties:
        type:
          type: string
          enum: [text, link, diff_summary, screenshot_ref, receipt_ref, test_result, change_ticket, artifact_ref, custom]
        label:
          type: string
        value:
          type: string

    AppendEvidenceRequest:
      type: object
      required: [evidence]
      properties:
        evidence:
          type: array
          items:
            $ref: "#/components/schemas/EvidenceItem"

    PolicyMatch:
      type: object
      properties:
        policy_id: { type: string }
        policy_version: { type: integer }
        rule_id: { type: string }
        explanation: { type: string }

    Decision:
      type: object
      required: [disposition, risk_level]
      properties:
        disposition:
          $ref: "#/components/schemas/DecisionDisposition"
        risk_level:
          $ref: "#/components/schemas/RiskLevel"
        matched_policies:
          type: array
          items:
            $ref: "#/components/schemas/PolicyMatch"
        requires_approval:
          type: boolean
        approval_id:
          type: string
          nullable: true
        execution_grant_id:
          type: string
          nullable: true
        denial_reason:
          type: string
          nullable: true

    IntentEnvelope:
      type: object
      required: [intent_id, status, workspace_id, agent, action, decision, created_at, updated_at]
      properties:
        intent_id:
          type: string
        workspace_id:
          type: string
        status:
          $ref: "#/components/schemas/IntentStatus"
        agent:
          $ref: "#/components/schemas/ActorRef"
        action:
          $ref: "#/components/schemas/ActionIntent"
        context:
          $ref: "#/components/schemas/IntentContext"
        decision:
          $ref: "#/components/schemas/Decision"
        evidence:
          type: array
          items:
            $ref: "#/components/schemas/EvidenceItem"
        created_at:
          type: string
          format: date-time
        updated_at:
          type: string
          format: date-time

    IntentListResponse:
      type: object
      properties:
        items:
          type: array
          items:
            $ref: "#/components/schemas/IntentEnvelope"
        pagination:
          $ref: "#/components/schemas/Pagination"

    ApprovalActor:
      type: object
      required: [principal_id]
      properties:
        principal_id:
          type: string
        display_name:
          type: string
        role:
          type: string
        status:
          type: string
          enum: [pending, approved, denied, delegated]

    Approval:
      type: object
      required: [approval_id, intent_id, status, requested_at, approvers]
      properties:
        approval_id:
          type: string
        intent_id:
          type: string
        workspace_id:
          type: string
        status:
          $ref: "#/components/schemas/ApprovalStatus"
        rationale:
          type: string
        approvers:
          type: array
          items:

        expires_at:
          type: string
          format: date-time
          nullable: true
        requested_at:
          type: string
          format: date-time
        resolved_at:
          type: string
          format: date-time
          nullable: true
        editable_bounds:
          type: object
          additionalProperties: true

    ApprovalListResponse:
      type: object
      properties:
        items:
          type: array
          items:
            $ref: "#/components/schemas/Approval"
        pagination:
          $ref: "#/components/schemas/Pagination"

    ApproveRequest:
      type: object
      properties:
        comment:
          type: string
        approve_once:
          type: boolean
          default: false

step_up_auth_assertion:
          type: string

    DenyRequest:
      type: object
      required: [reason]
      properties:
        reason:
          type: string
        comment:
          type: string

    ModifyApprovalRequest:
      type: object
      required: [modifications]
      properties:
        comment:
          type: string
        modifications:
          type: object
          additionalProperties: true

    ApprovalActionResponse:
      type: object
      required: [approval_id, status]
      properties:
        approval_id:
          type: string
        status:
          $ref: "#/components/schemas/ApprovalStatus"
        intent_status:
          $ref: "#/components/schemas/IntentStatus"
        execution_grant_id:
          type: string
          nullable: true

    PolicyCondition:
      type: object
      properties:
        field:
          type: string
        operator:
          type: string
          enum: [eq, neq, in, not_in, gt, gte, lt, lte, contains, matches]
        value:
          oneOf:
            - type: string
            - type: number
            - type: integer
            - type: boolean
            - type: array
              items: {}

    ApprovalRequirement:
      type: object
      properties:
        mode:
          type: string
          enum: [single, any_of_group, all_of_group, quorum]
        approver_group_id:
          type: string
        quorum_count:
          type: integer
          minimum: 1

    PolicyRule:
      type: object
      required: [rule_id, effect]
      properties:
        rule_id:
          type: string
        description:
          type: string
        conditions:
          type: array
          items:
            $ref: "#/components/schemas/PolicyCondition"
        effect:
          type: string
          enum: [allow, deny, require_approval, allow_with_modification]
        approval_requirement:
          $ref: "#/components/schemas/ApprovalRequirement"
        modifications_template:
          type: object
          additionalProperties: true
        priority:
          type: integer
          default: 100

    Policy:
      type: object
      required: [policy_id, name, workspace_id, status, version, rules]
      properties:
        policy_id:
          type: string
        workspace_id:
          type: string
        name:
          type: string
        description:
          type: string
        environment:
          type: string
        status:
          $ref: "#/components/schemas/PolicyStatus"
        version:
          type: integer
        rules:
          type: array
          items:
            $ref: "#/components/schemas/PolicyRule"
        created_at:
          type: string
          format: date-time
        updated_at:
          type: string
          format: date-time

    CreatePolicyRequest:
      type: object
      required: [workspace_id, name, rules]
      properties:
        workspace_id:
          type: string
        name:
          type: string
        description:
          type: string
        environment:
          type: string
        status:
          $ref: "#/components/schemas/PolicyStatus"
        rules:
          type: array
          items:
            $ref: "#/components/schemas/PolicyRule"

    UpdatePolicyRequest:
      type: object
      properties:
        name:
          type: string
        description:
          type: string
        status:
          $ref: "#/components/schemas/PolicyStatus"
        rules:
          type: array
          items:
            $ref: "#/components/schemas/PolicyRule"

    PolicyListResponse:
      type: object
      properties:
        items:
          type: array
          items:
            $ref: "#/components/schemas/Policy"
        pagination:
          $ref: "#/components/schemas/Pagination"

    PolicySimulationRequest:
      type: object
      required: [workspace_id, action]
      properties:
        workspace_id:
          type: string
        action:
          $ref: "#/components/schemas/ActionIntent"
        context:
          $ref: "#/components/schemas/IntentContext"

    PolicySimulationResponse:
      type: object
      required: [decision]
      properties:
        decision:
          $ref: "#/components/schemas/Decision"

      type: object
      required: [method]
      properties:

        reference_id:
          type: string
        scopes:
          type: array
          items:
            type: string

    Connector:
      type: object

      properties:

        workspace_id:
          type: string
        type:

        name:
          type: string
        provider:
          type: string
        status:
          type: string
          enum: [active, disabled, error]
        environment:
          type: string
        auth:
          $ref: "#/components/schemas/ConnectorAuth"
        policy_ids:
          type: array
          items:

        metadata:

          additionalProperties: true

    CreateConnectorRequest:

        type:

        name:

        provider:

        environment:

        auth:

        metadata:

        metadata:
          type: object

    ConnectorListResponse:

    ExecutionGrant:

          type: string

          type: string

        bounded_parameters:
          type: object
          additionalProperties: true
        expires_at:
          type: string
          format: date-time

    ExecutionRunRequest:
      type: object
      required: [grant_id]
      properties:
        grant_id:
          type: string
        override_parameters:
          type: object
          additionalProperties: true

    ExecutionRunResponse:

        status:

    Execution:

          type: string
        status:

    ExecutionCallbackRequest:

content.text: Now I have a thorough understanding of the entire codebase. Let me write my analysis.

input.content:
# Aileron Scaffold Analysis: Business Viability & Code/API Design Review

## 1. Business & Product Analysis

### Does this fit an emergent and important need?

**Yes — emphatically.** This sits at a genuine inflection point. Here's the case:

**The timing is right.** Agentic AI is moving from demo to production across enterprises. Every company experimenting with agents that touch real systems (payments, code, infrastructure) immediately hits the trust/control wall. The workarounds today — hardcoded rules in agent code, Slack-based approvals, over-permissioned API keys — are exactly what you describe, and they don't scale.

**The category is forming now.** There is no established "agentic control plane" product category yet. A few adjacent things exist:
- **Agent frameworks** (LangChain, CrewAI, AutoGen) handle orchestration but punt on governance
- **API gateways** (Kong, Apigee) handle auth/rate-limiting but don't understand intent semantics or human approval
- **Policy engines** (OPA, Cedar) evaluate rules but aren't packaged for the agentic use case
- **Spend management** (Brex, Ramp) handles corporate cards but isn't agent-aware

Nobody stitches these together into a coherent control plane purpose-built for agents. That's the gap.

**The wedge is strong.** Payments is an excellent entry wedge because:
- The pain is acute (agents spending money = immediate executive attention)
- The compliance requirements are non-negotiable (audit trails, approval flows)
- The monetization path is clear (transaction fees, per-seat, usage-based)
- It's a forcing function for the full lifecycle (intent → policy → approval → execution → audit)

**The OSS + SaaS model is the right play.** Open-source core builds trust and adoption in the infrastructure community (Go is the right language for this). SaaS overlay captures revenue from teams that don't want to operate it themselves. This mirrors the Terraform/HashiCorp, GitLab, PostHog playbook.

### Risks & considerations

- **Market timing vs. market readiness:** The number of companies with agents in production doing real actions (not just chat) is still small. You're building for where the market will be in 12-18 months, which is the right bet but requires runway.
- **Chicken-and-egg with agent frameworks:** Agents need to be modified to submit intents to Aileron (or Aileron needs to sit as a transparent proxy). The "no agent modification required" goal in your architecture notes is important but hard — the current API design requires agents to actively submit intents, which *is* a modification. Consider: MCP tool server, HTTP proxy mode, or agent framework plugins (LangChain tool, CrewAI middleware) as zero-modification integration paths.
- **Competitive moats:** The moat is the policy+approval+audit lifecycle combined with the connector ecosystem. Individual pieces are commoditizable. The integrated experience is not.
- **Enterprise readiness signals:** SOC 2, audit trails, RBAC, SSO. The audit store SPI is a great start. Multi-tenancy in the SaaS layer is critical and correctly deferred.

### Verdict: Strong product-market fit for the emerging category. The risk is adoption timing, not concept validity.

---

## 2. Code & API Design Review

### What's done well

**SPI architecture is excellent.** Every subsystem (policy, approval, connectors, vault, notify, audit) is defined as a narrow Go interface. This is textbook for an infrastructure product that needs pluggability. The interfaces are appropriately minimal — `Engine.Evaluate()`, `Connector.Execute()`, `Vault.Get/Put/Delete`, `Store.Append`. No interface has more methods than it needs.

**The intent lifecycle is well-modeled.** The flow `intent → policy decision → approval (optional) → execution grant → execution → trace` is a clean state machine. The OpenAPI spec models this as distinct resources with clear status enums, which makes it easy for SDK consumers to follow.

**Domain payloads are thoughtfully designed.** The `DomainAction` union type (git, deploy, cloud, email, calendar, payment, procurement) gives the policy engine rich context for evaluation without forcing a generic key-value model. Payment fields like `merchant_category`, `budget_code`, `line_items`, `beneficiary` show real domain understanding.

**Credential isolation is a first-class concern.** The `InjectedCredential` type on `ExecutionRequest`, the vault SPI, and the explicit documentation that connectors must not store/log credentials — this is the right level of paranoia for a system handling payments and API keys.

**The OpenAPI spec is comprehensive and production-grade.** 1833 lines covering all resources, proper pagination, idempotency keys, examples with realistic data, error schema, and evidence types. This is ahead of where most scaffolds are.

**Go workspace layout is clean.** Separate modules for `core` and `sdk/go` via `go.work`, allowing independent versioning. No unnecessary dependencies — the entire core uses only the standard library at this stage.

### Design issues & recommendations

#### API Design

1. **Missing workspace scoping on several endpoints.** `GET /v1/policies`, `GET /v1/connectors`, `GET /v1/funding-sources`, `GET /v1/credentials`, and `GET /v1/traces` don't require `workspace_id` as a query parameter. In a multi-tenant system, every list endpoint should be workspace-scoped (or the middleware should inject it from the auth token). The intents endpoint does this correctly — the others should follow.

2. **No error responses declared on endpoints.** The OpenAPI spec defines an `Error` schema but none of the endpoints reference it in their `responses` block. Add `400`, `401`, `403`, `404`, `409` responses as appropriate. This matters for SDK code generation and client-side error handling.

3. **`DomainAction` is a loosely-typed union.** All seven domain fields are optional with no discriminator. A `oneOf` with a discriminator on `action.type` would give better validation and cleaner code generation. Currently, nothing prevents submitting a `payment` domain payload with a `git.pull_request.create` action type.

4. **Execution grant lifecycle needs a `POST /v1/executions/run` → grant consumption link.** The `ExecutionRunRequest` takes a `grant_id`, but there's no explicit "consume" transition on the grant itself. The grant status enum includes `consumed`, so the server presumably does this internally, but consider whether the grant should be a sub-resource of the intent or have its own list endpoint for monitoring.

5. **`POST /v1/policies/simulate` conflicts with REST resource naming.** The path looks like creating a resource under `policies` with ID `simulate`. Consider `POST /v1/policy-simulations` as a separate resource, or keep it but document that it's an RPC-style endpoint.

6. **`modifications` on `ModifyApprovalRequest` is `additionalProperties: true`.** This is maximally flexible but gives no guidance on what can be modified. The `editable_bounds` concept on `Approval` is the right idea — consider making `modifications` a typed structure that mirrors the bounded parameters, or at least document the expected shape per domain.

7. **Missing webhook/callback registration endpoints.** The `POST /v1/executions/{id}/callback` endpoint exists for connectors to report results, but there's no way for agents to register a webhook URL to be notified when their intent progresses. Agents will need to poll. Consider adding a `callback_url` field to `CreateIntentRequest` or a separate webhook subscription endpoint.

#### Go Code Design

8. **Go SPI types diverge from OpenAPI types.** The Go `policy.DomainPayload` has 3 domain types (Payment, Git, Calendar) while the OpenAPI `DomainAction` has 7 (adding Deploy, Cloud, Email, Procurement). The Go `PaymentPayload` fields (`VendorName`, `AmountMinor`, `Currency`) don't match the OpenAPI `PaymentAction` structure (`vendor_name`, `amount` as `Money` object, `merchant_category`, `line_items`, `beneficiary`). These should be aligned — either generate Go types from the OpenAPI spec or keep them manually in sync.

9. **The `connector.Registry` is not safe for concurrent use.** The `connectors` map has no synchronization. If connectors are registered at startup only (as in `main.go`), this is fine. If the API allows runtime connector registration (which the `POST /v1/connectors` endpoint implies), you'll need a `sync.RWMutex` or a concurrent-safe map.

10. **`notify.Multi` error handling silently continues.** The `Multi` notifier collects errors from individual notifiers but still returns `nil` if some succeed — it only returns the `MultiError` if *all* fail (actually, it returns the error collection regardless, which is good). But the caller has no way to know which notifiers failed without type-asserting to `MultiError`. This is acceptable for notifications but worth documenting.

11. **No `context.Context` on `Registry.Get()` or `Registry.Register()`.** If the registry ever becomes database-backed (for runtime connector management), these will need context. Low priority for the scaffold.

12. **The server's `config` struct reads `DATABASE_URL` but doesn't use it.** Minor, but currently the database URL is loaded and discarded. When you add database integration, this is where the connection pool initialization should go.

#### Structural

13. **No shared `model` or `domain` package.** The `DomainPayload`, `ActionTarget`, and related types are defined in `core/policy/spi.go` but will also be needed by the approval orchestrator, connectors, audit store, and HTTP handlers. Consider a `core/model` or `core/domain` package to avoid circular imports when multiple subsystems need the same types.

14. **SDK client structure is good but consider code generation.** The Go SDK is hand-written, which is fine for now. But with a comprehensive OpenAPI spec, you could generate both Go and TypeScript SDKs, ensuring they stay in sync with the API as it evolves. Tools: `oapi-codegen` for Go, `openapi-typescript` for TS.

15. **No middleware layer in the server.** The `net/http` ServeMux is used directly with no middleware chain. You'll need auth (JWT validation), request ID injection, structured logging, CORS, and rate limiting. Consider a lightweight middleware approach (stdlib `http.Handler` wrapping is fine — no need for a framework).

### Summary scorecard

| Dimension | Rating | Notes |
|-----------|--------|-------|
| Problem/market fit | Strong | Category-defining timing, real pain point |
| Business model | Strong | OSS + SaaS is proven, payments wedge is monetizable |
| API design | Good | Comprehensive lifecycle, needs tightening on type unions and error responses |
| Go architecture | Very good | SPI pattern is clean and extensible |
| Code quality | Good | Idiomatic Go, clear documentation, appropriate use of stdlib |
| Scaffold completeness | Good | All surfaces defined, nothing critical missing for an MVP skeleton |
| Key risk | — | Go/OpenAPI type divergence will create bugs if not resolved before implementation |

**SPI architecture is excellent.** Every subsystem (policy, approval, connectors, vault, notify, audit) is defined as a narrow Go interface. This is textbook for an infrastructure product that needs pluggability. The interfaces are appropriately minimal — `Engine.Evaluate()`, `Connector.Execute()`, `Vault.Get/Put/Delete`, `Store.Append`. No interface has more methods than it needs.

**The intent lifecycle is well-modeled.** The flow `intent → policy decision → approval (optional) → execution grant → execution → trace` is a clean state machine. The OpenAPI spec models this as distinct resources with clear status enums, which makes it easy for SDK consumers to follow.

**Domain payloads are thoughtfully designed.** The `DomainAction` union type (git, deploy, cloud, email, calendar, payment, procurement) gives the policy engine rich context for evaluation without forcing a generic key-value model. Payment fields like `merchant_category`, `budget_code`, `line_items`, `beneficiary` show real domain understanding.

**Credential isolation is a first-class concern.** The `InjectedCredential` type on `ExecutionRequest`, the vault SPI, and the explicit documentation that connectors must not store/log credentials — this is the right level of paranoia for a system handling payments and API keys.

**The OpenAPI spec is comprehensive and production-grade.** 1833 lines covering all resources, proper pagination, idempotency keys, examples with realistic data, error schema, and evidence types. This is ahead of where most scaffolds are.

**Go workspace layout is clean.** Separate modules for `core` and `sdk/go` via `go.work`, allowing independent versioning. No unnecessary dependencies — the entire core uses only the standard library at this stage.

### Design issues & recommendations

#### API Design

1. **Missing workspace scoping on several endpoints.** `GET /v1/policies`, `GET /v1/connectors`, `GET /v1/funding-sources`, `GET /v1/credentials`, and `GET /v1/traces` don't require `workspace_id` as a query parameter. In a multi-tenant system, every list endpoint should be workspace-scoped (or the middleware should inject it from the auth token). The intents endpoint does this correctly — the others should follow.

2. **No error responses declared on endpoints.** The OpenAPI spec defines an `Error` schema but none of the endpoints reference it in their `responses` block. Add `400`, `401`, `403`, `404`, `409` responses as appropriate. This matters for SDK code generation and client-side error handling.

3. **`DomainAction` is a loosely-typed union.** All seven domain fields are optional with no discriminator. A `oneOf` with a discriminator on `action.type` would give better validation and cleaner code generation. Currently, nothing prevents submitting a `payment` domain payload with a `git.pull_request.create` action type.

4. **Execution grant lifecycle needs a `POST /v1/executions/run` → grant consumption link.** The `ExecutionRunRequest` takes a `grant_id`, but there's no explicit "consume" transition on the grant itself. The grant status enum includes `consumed`, so the server presumably does this internally, but consider whether the grant should be a sub-resource of the intent or have its own list endpoint for monitoring.

5. **`POST /v1/policies/simulate` conflicts with REST resource naming.** The path looks like creating a resource under `policies` with ID `simulate`. Consider `POST /v1/policy-simulations` as a separate resource, or keep it but document that it's an RPC-style endpoint.

6. **`modifications` on `ModifyApprovalRequest` is `additionalProperties: true`.** This is maximally flexible but gives no guidance on what can be modified. The `editable_bounds` concept on `Approval` is the right idea — consider making `modifications` a typed structure that mirrors the bounded parameters, or at least document the expected shape per domain.

7. **Missing webhook/callback registration endpoints.** The `POST /v1/executions/{id}/callback` endpoint exists for connectors to report results, but there's no way for agents to register a webhook URL to be notified when their intent progresses. Agents will need to poll. Consider adding a `callback_url` field to `CreateIntentRequest` or a separate webhook subscription endpoint.

#### Go Code Design

8. **Go SPI types diverge from OpenAPI types.** The Go `policy.DomainPayload` has 3 domain types (Payment, Git, Calendar) while the OpenAPI `DomainAction` has 7 (adding Deploy, Cloud, Email, Procurement). The Go `PaymentPayload` fields (`VendorName`, `AmountMinor`, `Currency`) don't match the OpenAPI `PaymentAction` structure (`vendor_name`, `amount` as `Money` object, `merchant_category`, `line_items`, `beneficiary`). These should be aligned — either generate Go types from the OpenAPI spec or keep them manually in sync.

9. **The `connector.Registry` is not safe for concurrent use.** The `connectors` map has no synchronization. If connectors are registered at startup only (as in `main.go`), this is fine. If the API allows runtime connector registration (which the `POST /v1/connectors` endpoint implies), you'll need a `sync.RWMutex` or a concurrent-safe map.

10. **`notify.Multi` error handling silently continues.** The `Multi` notifier collects errors from individual notifiers but still returns `nil` if some succeed — it only returns the `MultiError` if *all* fail (actually, it returns the error collection regardless, which is good). But the caller has no way to know which notifiers failed without type-asserting to `MultiError`. This is acceptable for notifications but worth documenting.

11. **No `context.Context` on `Registry.Get()` or `Registry.Register()`.** If the registry ever becomes database-backed (for runtime connector management), these will need context. Low priority for the scaffold.

12. **The server's `config` struct reads `DATABASE_URL` but doesn't use it.** Minor, but currently the database URL is loaded and discarded. When you add database integration, this is where the connection pool initialization should go.

#### Structural

13. **No shared `model` or `domain` package.** The `DomainPayload`, `ActionTarget`, and related types are defined in `core/policy/spi.go` but will also be needed by the approval orchestrator, connectors, audit store, and HTTP handlers. Consider a `core/model` or `core/domain` package to avoid circular imports when multiple subsystems need the same types.

14. **SDK client structure is good but consider code generation.** The Go SDK is hand-written, which is fine for now. But with a comprehensive OpenAPI spec, you could generate both Go and TypeScript SDKs, ensuring they stay in sync with the API as it evolves. Tools: `oapi-codegen` for Go, `openapi-typescript` for TS.

15. **No middleware layer in the server.** The `net/http` ServeMux is used directly with no middleware chain. You'll need auth (JWT validation), request ID injection, structured logging, CORS, and rate limiting. Consider a lightweight middleware approach (stdlib `http.Handler` wrapping is fine — no need for a framework).

### Summary scorecard

| Dimension | Rating | Notes |
|-----------|--------|-------|
| Problem/market fit | Strong | Category-defining timing, real pain point |
| Business model | Strong | OSS + SaaS is proven, payments wedge is monetizable |
| API design | Good | Comprehensive lifecycle, needs tightening on type unions and error responses |
| Go architecture | Very good | SPI pattern is clean and extensible |
| Code quality | Good | Idiomatic Go, clear documentation, appropriate use of stdlib |
| Scaffold completeness | Good | All surfaces defined, nothing critical missing for an MVP skeleton |
| Key risk | — | Go/OpenAPI type divergence will create bugs if not resolved before implementation |

# Aileron Scaffold Analysis: Business Viability & Code/API Design Review

## 1. Business & Product Analysis

### Does this fit an emergent and important need?

**Yes — emphatically.** This sits at a genuine inflection point. Here's the case:

**The timing is right.** Agentic AI is moving from demo to production across enterprises. Every company experimenting with agents that touch real systems (payments, code, infrastructure) immediately hits the trust/control wall. The workarounds today — hardcoded rules in agent code, Slack-based approvals, over-permissioned API keys — are exactly what you describe, and they don't scale.

**The category is forming now.** There is no established "agentic control plane" product category yet. A few adjacent things exist:
- **Agent frameworks** (LangChain, CrewAI, AutoGen) handle orchestration but punt on governance
- **API gateways** (Kong, Apigee) handle auth/rate-limiting but don't understand intent semantics or human approval
- **Policy engines** (OPA, Cedar) evaluate rules but aren't packaged for the agentic use case
- **Spend management** (Brex, Ramp) handles corporate cards but isn't agent-aware

Nobody stitches these together into a coherent control plane purpose-built for agents. That's the gap.

**The wedge is strong.** Payments is an excellent entry wedge because:
- The pain is acute (agents spending money = immediate executive attention)
- The compliance requirements are non-negotiable (audit trails, approval flows)
- The monetization path is clear (transaction fees, per-seat, usage-based)
- It's a forcing function for the full lifecycle (intent → policy → approval → execution → audit)

**The OSS + SaaS model is the right play.** Open-source core builds trust and adoption in the infrastructure community (Go is the right language for this). SaaS overlay captures revenue from teams that don't want to operate it themselves. This mirrors the Terraform/HashiCorp, GitLab, PostHog playbook.

### Risks & considerations

- **Market timing vs. market readiness:** The number of companies with agents in production doing real actions (not just chat) is still small. You're building for where the market will be in 12-18 months, which is the right bet but requires runway.
- **Chicken-and-egg with agent frameworks:** Agents need to be modified to submit intents to Aileron (or Aileron needs to sit as a transparent proxy). The "no agent modification required" goal in your architecture notes is important but hard — the current API design requires agents to actively submit intents, which *is* a modification. Consider: MCP tool server, HTTP proxy mode, or agent framework plugins (LangChain tool, CrewAI middleware) as zero-modification integration paths.
- **Competitive moats:** The moat is the policy+approval+audit lifecycle combined with the connector ecosystem. Individual pieces are commoditizable. The integrated experience is not.
- **Enterprise readiness signals:** SOC 2, audit trails, RBAC, SSO. The audit store SPI is a great start. Multi-tenancy in the SaaS layer is critical and correctly deferred.

### Verdict: Strong product-market fit for the emerging category. The risk is adoption timing, not concept validity.

- The monetization path is clear (transaction fees, per-seat, usage-based)
- It's a forcing function for the full lifecycle (intent → policy → approval → execution → audit)

**The OSS + SaaS model is the right play.** Open-source core builds trust and adoption in the infrastructure community (Go is the right language for this). SaaS overlay captures revenue from teams that don't want to operate it themselves. This mirrors the Terraform/HashiCorp, GitLab, PostHog playbook.

### Risks & considerations

- **Market timing vs. market readiness:** The number of companies with agents in production doing real actions (not just chat) is still small. You're building for where the market will be in 12-18 months, which is the right bet but requires runway.
- **Chicken-and-egg with agent frameworks:** Agents need to be modified to submit intents to Aileron (or Aileron needs to sit as a transparent proxy). The "no agent modification required" goal in your architecture notes is important but hard — the current API design requires agents to actively submit intents, which *is* a modification. Consider: MCP tool server, HTTP proxy mode, or agent framework plugins (LangChain tool, CrewAI middleware) as zero-modification integration paths.
- **Competitive moats:** The moat is the policy+approval+audit lifecycle combined with the connector ecosystem. Individual pieces are commoditizable. The integrated experience is not.
- **Enterprise readiness signals:** SOC 2, audit trails, RBAC, SSO. The audit store SPI is a great start. Multi-tenancy in the SaaS layer is critical and correctly deferred.

### Verdict: Strong product-market fit for the emerging category. The risk is adoption timing, not concept validity.

---

## 2. Code & API Design Review

### What's done well

**SPI architecture is excellent.** Every subsystem (policy, approval, connectors, vault, notify, audit) is defined as a narrow Go interface. This is textbook for an infrastructure product that needs pluggability. The interfaces are appropriately minimal — `Engine.Evaluate()`, `Connector.Execute()`, `Vault.Get/Put/Delete`, `Store.Append`. No interface has more methods than it needs.

**The intent lifecycle is well-modeled.** The flow `intent → policy decision → approval (optional) → execution grant → execution → trace` is a clean state machine. The OpenAPI spec models this as distinct resources with clear status enums, which makes it easy for SDK consumers to follow.

**Domain payloads are thoughtfully designed.** The `DomainAction` union type (git, deploy, cloud, email, calendar, payment, procurement) gives the policy engine rich context for evaluation without forcing a generic key-value model. Payment fields like `merchant_category`, `budget_code`, `line_items`, `beneficiary` show real domain understanding.

**Credential isolation is a first-class concern.** The `InjectedCredential` type on `ExecutionRequest`, the vault SPI, and the explicit documentation that connectors must not store/log credentials — this is the right level of paranoia for a system handling payments and API keys.

**The OpenAPI spec is comprehensive and production-grade.** 1833 lines covering all resources, proper pagination, idempotency keys, examples with realistic data, error schema, and evidence types. This is ahead of where most scaffolds are.

**Go workspace layout is clean.** Separate modules for `core` and `sdk/go` via `go.work`, allowing independent versioning. No unnecessary dependencies — the entire core uses only the standard library at this stage.

### Design issues & recommendations

#### API Design

1. **Missing workspace scoping on several endpoints.** `GET /v1/policies`, `GET /v1/connectors`, `GET /v1/funding-sources`, `GET /v1/credentials`, and `GET /v1/traces` don't require `workspace_id` as a query parameter. In a multi-tenant system, every list endpoint should be workspace-scoped (or the middleware should inject it from the auth token). The intents endpoint does this correctly — the others should follow.

2. **No error responses declared on endpoints.** The OpenAPI spec defines an `Error` schema but none of the endpoints reference it in their `responses` block. Add `400`, `401`, `403`, `404`, `409` responses as appropriate. This matters for SDK code generation and client-side error handling.

3. **`DomainAction` is a loosely-typed union.** All seven domain fields are optional with no discriminator. A `oneOf` with a discriminator on `action.type` would give better validation and cleaner code generation. Currently, nothing prevents submitting a `payment` domain payload with a `git.pull_request.create` action type.

4. **Execution grant lifecycle needs a `POST /v1/executions/run` → grant consumption link.** The `ExecutionRunRequest` takes a `grant_id`, but there's no explicit "consume" transition on the grant itself. The grant status enum includes `consumed`, so the server presumably does this internally, but consider whether the grant should be a sub-resource of the intent or have its own list endpoint for monitoring.

5. **`POST /v1/policies/simulate` conflicts with REST resource naming.** The path looks like creating a resource under `policies` with ID `simulate`. Consider `POST /v1/policy-simulations` as a separate resource, or keep it but document that it's an RPC-style endpoint.

6. **`modifications` on `ModifyApprovalRequest` is `additionalProperties: true`.** This is maximally flexible but gives no guidance on what can be modified. The `editable_bounds` concept on `Approval` is the right idea — consider making `modifications` a typed structure that mirrors the bounded parameters, or at least document the expected shape per domain.

7. **Missing webhook/callback registration endpoints.** The `POST /v1/executions/{id}/callback` endpoint exists for connectors to report results, but there's no way for agents to register a webhook URL to be notified when their intent progresses. Agents will need to poll. Consider adding a `callback_url` field to `CreateIntentRequest` or a separate webhook subscription endpoint.

#### Go Code Design

8. **Go SPI types diverge from OpenAPI types.** The Go `policy.DomainPayload` has 3 domain types (Payment, Git, Calendar) while the OpenAPI `DomainAction` has 7 (adding Deploy, Cloud, Email, Procurement). The Go `PaymentPayload` fields (`VendorName`, `AmountMinor`, `Currency`) don't match the OpenAPI `PaymentAction` structure (`vendor_name`, `amount` as `Money` object, `merchant_category`, `line_items`, `beneficiary`). These should be aligned — either generate Go types from the OpenAPI spec or keep them manually in sync.

9. **The `connector.Registry` is not safe for concurrent use.** The `connectors` map has no synchronization. If connectors are registered at startup only (as in `main.go`), this is fine. If the API allows runtime connector registration (which the `POST /v1/connectors` endpoint implies), you'll need a `sync.RWMutex` or a concurrent-safe map.

10. **`notify.Multi` error handling silently continues.** The `Multi` notifier collects errors from individual notifiers but still returns `nil` if some succeed — it only returns the `MultiError` if *all* fail (actually, it returns the error collection regardless, which is good). But the caller has no way to know which notifiers failed without type-asserting to `MultiError`. This is acceptable for notifications but worth documenting.

11. **No `context.Context` on `Registry.Get()` or `Registry.Register()`.** If the registry ever becomes database-backed (for runtime connector management), these will need context. Low priority for the scaffold.

12. **The server's `config` struct reads `DATABASE_URL` but doesn't use it.** Minor, but currently the database URL is loaded and discarded. When you add database integration, this is where the connection pool initialization should go.

#### Structural

13. **No shared `model` or `domain` package.** The `DomainPayload`, `ActionTarget`, and related types are defined in `core/policy/spi.go` but will also be needed by the approval orchestrator, connectors, audit store, and HTTP handlers. Consider a `core/model` or `core/domain` package to avoid circular imports when multiple subsystems need the same types.

14. **SDK client structure is good but consider code generation.** The Go SDK is hand-written, which is fine for now. But with a comprehensive OpenAPI spec, you could generate both Go and TypeScript SDKs, ensuring they stay in sync with the API as it evolves. Tools: `oapi-codegen` for Go, `openapi-typescript` for TS.

15. **No middleware layer in the server.** The `net/http` ServeMux is used directly with no middleware chain. You'll need auth (JWT validation), request ID injection, structured logging, CORS, and rate limiting. Consider a lightweight middleware approach (stdlib `http.Handler` wrapping is fine — no need for a framework).

### Summary scorecard

| Dimension | Rating | Notes |
|-----------|--------|-------|
| Problem/market fit | Strong | Category-defining timing, real pain point |
| Business model | Strong | OSS + SaaS is proven, payments wedge is monetizable |
| API design | Good | Comprehensive lifecycle, needs tightening on type unions and error responses |
| Go architecture | Very good | SPI pattern is clean and extensible |
| Code quality | Good | Idiomatic Go, clear documentation, appropriate use of stdlib |
| Scaffold completeness | Good | All surfaces defined, nothing critical missing for an MVP skeleton |
| Key risk | — | Go/OpenAPI type divergence will create bugs if not resolved before implementation |

content.text:
The analysis is complete. The plan file has the full writeup, but the bottom line:

**Business:** You're in the right place at the right time. The agentic control plane category is forming and nobody owns it yet. Payments as the entry wedge is smart — it forces the full lifecycle and gets budget-holder attention.

**Code:** The scaffold is well above average. The SPI architecture, intent lifecycle, and OpenAPI spec are all strong. The most important thing to fix before building on top of this is **item #8 — the Go/OpenAPI type divergence** (3 domain types in Go vs. 7 in the spec, mismatched field structures). That will compound into real bugs if not resolved early. A shared `core/model` package (#13) would also pay for itself quickly.

Want me to start addressing any of the 15 findings?
Let me set up tasks and start working through the findings systematically, starting with the structural foundation.

1→// Package vault defines the SPI for credential and secret storage.
     2→//
     3→// The vault stores sensitive values (API keys, OAuth tokens, service account
     4→// credentials) and injects them into connector execution requests without
     5→// ever exposing them to agents. Agents see only a credential reference ID;
     6→// the vault resolves the actual secret at execution time.
     7→//
     8→// The built-in implementation encrypts secrets at rest using AES-GCM with
     9→// a key stored in the environment. Alternative implementations can delegate
    10→// to HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, GCP Secret Manager, etc.
    11→package vault
    12→
    13→import "context"
    14→
    15→// Vault stores and retrieves secrets by path.
    16→type Vault interface {
    17→	// Get retrieves a secret by its vault path.
    18→	Get(ctx context.Context, path string) (Secret, error)
    19→
    20→	// Put stores a secret at the given path, creating or overwriting it.
    21→	Put(ctx context.Context, path string, value []byte, meta Metadata) error
    22→
    23→	// Delete permanently removes a secret.
    24→	Delete(ctx context.Context, path string) error
    25→}
    26→
    27→// Secret is a resolved secret value.
    28→type Secret struct {
    29→	Path     string
    30→	Value    []byte
    31→	Metadata Metadata
    32→}
    33→
    34→// Metadata carries non-secret attributes stored alongside a secret.
    35→type Metadata struct {
    36→	// Type classifies the credential (e.g. "api_key", "oauth_refresh_token").
    37→	Type        string
    38→	Environment string
    39→	// Labels are arbitrary key-value pairs for organization.
    40→	Labels map[string]string
    41→}
    42→

1→// Package audit defines the SPI for the immutable audit/trace store.
     2→//
     3→// Every significant event in the control plane lifecycle — intent submission,
     4→// policy decision, approval action, execution outcome — is recorded as an
     5→// append-only trace event. This provides a verifiable audit trail for
     6→// compliance, debugging, and trust.
     7→//
     8→// The built-in implementation writes to a Postgres append-only table.
     9→// Alternative implementations can target immutable object storage, a
    10→// dedicated audit log service, or a blockchain-backed store.
    11→package audit
    12→
    13→import (
    14→	"context"
    15→	"time"
    16→)
    17→
    18→// Store records and retrieves immutable trace events.
    19→type Store interface {
    20→	// Append records a new event. Implementations must treat this as
    21→	// append-only: existing events must never be modified or deleted.
    22→	Append(ctx context.Context, event Event) error
    23→
    24→	// ListTraces returns traces matching the filter.
    25→	ListTraces(ctx context.Context, filter Filter) ([]Trace, error)
    26→
    27→	// GetTrace returns the full trace for a given intent.
    28→	GetTrace(ctx context.Context, traceID string) (Trace, error)
    29→}
    30→
    31→// Event is a single immutable audit record.
    32→type Event struct {
    33→	EventID   string
    34→	EventType EventType
    35→	Actor     ActorRef
    36→	// Payload carries event-specific data. Must be serialisable to JSON.
    37→	Payload   map[string]any
    38→	Timestamp time.Time
    39→}
    40→
    41→// ActorRef identifies who or what produced the event.
    42→type ActorRef struct {
    43→	ID          string
    44→	Type        ActorType
    45→	DisplayName string
    46→}
    47→
    48→// ActorType classifies the event producer.
    49→type ActorType string
    50→
    51→const (
    52→	ActorTypeAgent            ActorType = "agent"
    53→	ActorTypeHuman            ActorType = "human"
    54→	ActorTypeService          ActorType = "service"
    55→	ActorTypeConnectorRuntime ActorType = "connector_runtime"
    56→)
    57→
    58→// EventType identifies the kind of event.
    59→type EventType string
    60→
    61→const (
    62→	EventTypeIntentSubmitted    EventType = "intent.submitted"
    63→	EventTypePolicyEvaluated    EventType = "policy.evaluated"
    64→	EventTypeApprovalRequested  EventType = "approval.requested"
    65→	EventTypeApprovalApproved   EventType = "approval.approved"
    66→	EventTypeApprovalDenied     EventType = "approval.denied"
    67→	EventTypeApprovalModified   EventType = "approval.modified"
    68→	EventTypeGrantIssued        EventType = "grant.issued"
    69→	EventTypeGrantRevoked       EventType = "grant.revoked"
    70→	EventTypeExecutionStarted   EventType = "execution.started"
    71→	EventTypeExecutionSucceeded EventType = "execution.succeeded"
    72→	EventTypeExecutionFailed    EventType = "execution.failed"
    73→)
    74→
    75→// Trace is the full ordered event history for one intent.
    76→type Trace struct {
    77→	TraceID     string
    78→	IntentID    string
    79→	WorkspaceID string
    80→	Events      []Event
    81→}
    82→
    83→// Filter scopes a trace list query.
    84→type Filter struct {
    85→	WorkspaceID string
    86→	IntentID    string
    87→	ActorID     string
    88→	EventType   EventType
    89→	From        *time.Time
    90→	To          *time.Time
    91→	PageSize    int
    92→	PageToken   string
    93→}
    94→

1→// Package stripe implements the payments connector for Stripe.
     2→//
     3→// This connector executes payment.charge actions via the Stripe API using
     4→// Stripe Issuing virtual cards. Credentials are injected at execution time
     5→// from the vault and are never stored by this package.
     6→package stripe
     7→
     8→import (
     9→	"context"
    10→	"errors"
    11→
    12→	"github.com/ALRubinger/aileron/core/connector"
    13→)
    14→
    15→const (
    16→	connectorType     = "payments"
    17→	connectorProvider = "stripe"
    18→)
    19→
    20→// Connector executes payment actions via Stripe.
    21→type Connector struct{}
    22→
    23→// New returns a new Stripe payments connector.
    24→func New() *Connector {
    25→	return &Connector{}
    26→}
    27→
    28→// Type implements connector.Connector.
    29→func (c *Connector) Type() string { return connectorType }
    30→
    31→// Provider implements connector.Connector.
    32→func (c *Connector) Provider() string { return connectorProvider }
    33→
    34→// Execute implements connector.Connector.
    35→func (c *Connector) Execute(ctx context.Context, req connector.ExecutionRequest) (connector.ExecutionResult, error) {
    36→	if req.Credential == nil {
    37→		return connector.ExecutionResult{}, errors.New("stripe: no credential injected")
    38→	}
    39→
    40→	switch req.ActionType {
    41→	case "payment.charge":
    42→		return c.charge(ctx, req)
    43→	default:
    44→		return connector.ExecutionResult{
    45→			Status: connector.ExecutionStatusFailed,
    46→			Error:  "stripe: unsupported action type: " + req.ActionType,
    47→		}, nil
    48→	}
    49→}
    50→
    51→func (c *Connector) charge(_ context.Context, req connector.ExecutionRequest) (connector.ExecutionResult, error) {
    52→	// TODO: implement Stripe Issuing virtual card charge via Stripe API.
    53→	// Steps:
    54→	//   1. Resolve funding source to a Stripe Issuing cardholder / card pool.
    55→	//   2. Create an ephemeral virtual card scoped to the approved parameters.
    56→	//   3. Initiate the charge.
    57→	//   4. Return the Stripe charge ID as ReceiptRef.
    58→	_ = req
    59→	return connector.ExecutionResult{}, errors.New("stripe: charge not yet implemented")
    60→}
    61→
